evals.prompt: I'm deploying a stateless Node.js API to my production namespace. I want zero-downtime rolling updates and to make sure traffic isn't routed to a Pod that hasn't finished starting up. Write a production-ready Deployment manifest.
evals.expected_output: A Deployment manifest with maxUnavailable: 0, a readinessProbe, minReadySeconds set to 10-30s, and a kubernetes.io/change-cause annotation. The response explains that maxUnavailable: 0 prevents dropping below replica count during rollout, and that a readinessProbe is required to avoid routing traffic to unready Pods — minReadySeconds alone does not eliminate this window.
expectations: Sets maxUnavailable: 0 in the rollingUpdate strategy and explains this prevents the replica count from dropping during rollout
Defines a readinessProbe (not just a livenessProbe or minReadySeconds alone) and states that without it traffic is routed to Pods that have started but may not yet be serving
Sets minReadySeconds to a non-zero value (10-30s) and explains it reduces but does not eliminate the unready-traffic window
Includes a kubernetes.io/change-cause annotation to populate rollout history
Warns that with maxUnavailable: 0 a rollout hangs indefinitely if the new Pod never becomes ready, and recommends progressDeadlineSeconds plus kubectl rollout undo to recover

evals.prompt: I need to run a one-time database migration as part of my deployment pipeline. The migration must complete successfully before the new app version starts, and I need to be able to inspect logs if it fails. What Kubernetes resource should I use and how should I configure it?
evals.expected_output: A Job manifest with restartPolicy: Never, backoffLimit: 1, ttlSecondsAfterFinished set, and completionMode explained. The response explains why Never is preferred over OnFailure for log retention, and why ttlSecondsAfterFinished must always be set to prevent accumulation of completed Jobs.
expectations: Recommends a Job (not a Deployment or init container) as the correct resource for a one-time task that runs to completion
Sets restartPolicy: Never (not OnFailure) and explains that Never keeps the failed Pod for log inspection while OnFailure restarts in-place and may overwrite logs
Sets ttlSecondsAfterFinished and explains that without it completed Jobs and their Pods accumulate until manually deleted
Sets backoffLimit to a low value (0 or 1) appropriate for a migration, not the default of 6
Avoids recommending restartPolicy: Always, which is invalid for Jobs and only valid for Deployments

evals.prompt: I have a CronJob that backs up a database every 4 hours. Sometimes the backup takes longer than 4 hours and I end up with two backup Jobs running at the same time, which corrupts the backup. How do I fix this, and how do I test the CronJob immediately without waiting for the schedule?
evals.expected_output: Set concurrencyPolicy: Forbid on the CronJob to skip a new Job if the previous one is still running. To test immediately: kubectl create job --from=cronjob/<name> <test-job-name> -n <namespace>. The response also recommends increasing failedJobsHistoryLimit above the default of 1 to retain failure logs.
expectations: Sets concurrencyPolicy: Forbid (not Allow or Replace) and explains it skips the new Job if the previous is still running, which is correct for backups that must not overlap
Provides the exact kubectl create job --from=cronjob/<name> command to trigger a Job immediately without waiting for the schedule
Increases failedJobsHistoryLimit above the default of 1 (recommends 10) to retain enough failed Job logs for inspection
Distinguishes concurrencyPolicy: Replace (kills the previous Job) from Forbid (skips the new one), explaining why Replace is wrong for backups
Avoids recommending a Deployment or any resource other than CronJob for the recurring scheduled task
